executive_summary: Investors are optimistic but uneasy — a combination that tends to make markets interesting. In January 2026, more than 8 in 10 institutional managers and nearly 3 in 4 individual investors expected stock prices to be higher a year from now. Yet most of those same people think a crash is plausible within months, and individual investors almost unanimously believe stocks are overpriced. Call it the party guest who's having a great time but keeps an eye on the exit.

key_findings.headline: Broadly Bullish
key_findings.interpretation: More than 4 in 5 institutional investors — and nearly 3 in 4 individuals — expect the market to be higher a year from now. That's a strong baseline of confidence, even as concerns simmer in other areas.

key_findings.headline: Crash Worry Persists
key_findings.interpretation: Only about 1 in 4 investors feel confident a crash won't happen in the next six months. This isn't a panic reading — but it does mean most investors are living with real uncertainty, even as they stay invested.

key_findings.headline: Retail Eager, Wall Street Cautious
key_findings.interpretation: Individual investors are nearly twice as likely as institutional managers to say they'd buy into a market dip. This divergence can fuel short-term rallies after drops — but it also puts more market support in retail hands, which can be less stable.

key_findings.headline: Individuals: This Market Is Expensive
key_findings.interpretation: Just 7.7% of individual investors believe stocks are fairly or undervalued — one of the lowest readings on record. Meanwhile, 58% of institutional investors think valuations are reasonable. That's a historically wide gap between the two groups.

detailed_analysis:
The central tension in January's data is worth sitting with: investors are broadly optimistic about where the market is headed over the next year, yet most are also skeptical of current valuations and wary of a crash.

Among individual investors, the valuation reading is striking. Only 7.7% believe stocks are fairly or undervalued — one of the lowest readings in the survey's history. Yet 72.7% of those same investors expect prices to rise over the next year. One reading of this is behavioral: investors may believe the market will keep rising even as they sense it's gotten expensive. Another is simply that people distinguish between short-run momentum and long-run fair value — expecting prices to drift higher in the near term while acknowledging they're stretched.

Institutional investors offer a different perspective on valuation. While they're similarly bullish on the one-year outlook (81.8%), 58.3% of them see current valuations as reasonable. This could reflect a number of things: longer investment horizons, different valuation frameworks, or, for some, a belief that markets are fundamentally efficient — that the current price already reflects all available information, making the question of "overvaluation" less meaningful. Under that view, the market is always priced correctly by definition.

The buy-on-dips gap is also notable. Individual investors are nearly twice as likely as institutional managers to say they'd step in after a market pullback (76.9% vs. 45.5%). What exactly this signals is hard to say with confidence — but it does suggest the two groups have meaningfully different instincts when it comes to near-term volatility.
market_context: The January 2026 survey was conducted against a backdrop of mixed signals. Equity markets started the year with moderate volatility as investors processed Federal Reserve commentary on the pace of rate adjustments. Technology sector earnings were a mixed bag — strong revenue growth but margin pressure from rising costs. Bond yields remained elevated relative to pre-2022 levels, which may help explain why individual investors are so skeptical of current stock prices: higher yields make future earnings less valuable today, and they give investors a real alternative to equities for the first time in years. Geopolitical uncertainty continued to weigh on sentiment, consistent with the elevated crash concern readings across both investor groups.
methodology: These indices come from the Yale/Shiller U.S. Stock Market Confidence Surveys, which have tracked investor sentiment since 1989. Each month, roughly 100 wealthy individual investors and 100 institutional investment managers answer the same standardized questions. The consistency of those questions over 35+ years is what makes the data meaningful — you can actually compare investor psychology across very different market environments. The four indices each measure something distinct: • 1-Year Confidence: The share who expect prices to be higher in 12 months. • Crash Confidence: The share who believe a crash in the next 6 months is very unlikely (under 10% probability). A low reading means most people think a crash is plausible. • Buy-on-Dips Confidence: The share who believe that after a 3% single-day market drop, prices would fully recover within two days. • Valuation Confidence: The share who do not believe the market is overvalued. Surveys were conducted semi-annually before 2001 and have been monthly since July 2001. Respondents rotate over time, but consistent characteristics — high income, investment experience — are maintained across the panel.
